items.question: This time tomorrow, I ___ to Rome.
explanations: 'Will fly' is a single future action; here we describe being in the middle of the flight.
Correct! An action in progress at a future moment takes the future continuous: 'will be flying'.
'Fly' is present simple (a habit or timetable), not this future moment.
'Am flying' is a present arrangement, but 'this time tomorrow' needs the future continuous.

items.question: At 8pm, we ___ dinner.
explanations: 'Will have' is a single action; the point is the meal in progress at 8pm.
Correct! An action in progress at a future time takes the future continuous: 'will be having'.
'Have' is present simple, not this future moment.
'Had' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: ___ you ___ the car tonight?
explanations: Correct! A polite enquiry about plans uses the future continuous: 'Will you be using…?'.
'Do you use' asks about a habit, not tonight's plan.
After 'Will you' you need 'be' before the -ing form: 'be using'.
'Are you use' mixes structures. Use 'Will you be using…?'.

items.question: She ___ working late tomorrow.
explanations: 'Will working' is missing 'be'. Use 'will be working'.
Correct! The future continuous is will be + -ing: 'will be working'.
'Is working' is a present arrangement; 'tomorrow' with in-progress meaning takes 'will be'.
'Won't' is negative and changes the meaning.

items.question: Don't call at nine — I ___ then.
explanations: 'Will study' is a single action; here we mean being in the middle of studying at nine.
Correct! An action in progress at a future time takes the future continuous: 'will be studying'.
'Study' is present simple, not this future moment.
'Studied' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: By then, I ___ the answer.
explanations: 'Know' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous.
Correct! State verbs take the simple: 'will know'.
'Know' is present simple, but 'by then' points to the future.
'Knew' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: They ___ coming to the meeting.
explanations: Correct! Future continuous negative = won't be + -ing: 'won't be coming'.
'Don't be coming' isn't correct English.
'Aren't be' is not a valid form.
'Not will' has the wrong word order. Use 'won't be'.

items.question: I ___ Sara tomorrow anyway, so I'll pass on the message.
explanations: Correct! For something expected to happen as part of the future, the future continuous is natural: 'I'll be seeing Sara'.
'See' is present simple (a habit), not this future event.
'Saw' is past simple, but this is about tomorrow.
'Am seeing' is a fixed arrangement; the future continuous fits the 'anyway' meaning better here.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'At midnight, I'll be asleep.'
options: I'll go to sleep at midnight.
explanations: 'Go to sleep at midnight' is the moment of falling asleep, not being asleep.
Correct! 'Be asleep' at that moment = the future continuous 'will be sleeping'.
'I sleep at midnight' is a present habit.
'I slept' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: What ___ you ___ this time next week?
explanations: Correct! Asking about an action in progress at a future time uses the future continuous: 'will you be doing'.
'Do you do' asks about a habit, not next week.
'Are you doing' is a present arrangement, but 'this time next week' needs the future continuous.
After 'will you' you need 'be': 'will you be doing'.
